name: Tito Puente
abstract: Ernest Anthony Puente Jr. (April 20, 1923 – May 31, 2000), commonly known as Tito Puente, was an American musician, songwriter, bandleader, timbalero, vibraphonist and record producer.

about_quotes.speaker: Doc Cheatham
about_quotes.evidence: The demise of the big bands resulted in a new career for Doc, as he became a trumpet star with the Latin bands of Machito and Tito Puente.
about_quotes.doc_title: Doc Cheatham interviewed by Monk Rowe and Michael Woods, New York City, New York, July 28, 1995

about_quotes.speaker: Frank "Machito" Grillo
about_quotes.evidence: Tito Puente came with us and played about one year and a half or two years.

about_quotes.speaker: Ray Shiner
about_quotes.evidence: I played with Tito Puente, Tito Rodriguez, Sonny Dunham, a bunch of bands.
about_quotes.doc_title: Ray Shiner interviewed by Monk Rowe, Clinton, New York, October 11, 1998

about_quotes.speaker: Vince Lateano
about_quotes.evidence: The interviewee mentions Tito Puente only as a third party in a story about Cal Tjader ('he was here with Tito Puente once') and describes Puente playing at the Palladium, but does not describe a direct personal or professional relationship between himself and Puente.
about_quotes.doc_title: Vince Lateano interviewed by Monk Rowe, San Francisco, California, August 8, 2002

about_quotes.speaker: Mose Allison
about_quotes.evidence: The interviewer mentions Tito Puente in the context of music style ('like Tito Puente'), but Mose Allison does not respond with any personal interaction, meeting, or collaboration details.
about_quotes.doc_title: Mose Allison — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Wilson "Chembo" Corniel
about_quotes.evidence: So, and you know you’ve got a big band, Tito Puente, five saxes, four trombones, four trumpets, timbale, bongo, two singers you know, so it’s a full force.
about_quotes.doc_title: Wilson "Chembo" Corniel interviewed by Monk Rowe, Clinton, New York, November 15, 2019
